_comment: Per-organization qualitative findings. Update these whenever adding new pitchers shifts the narrative. Orgs with 3+ pitchers have fuller write-ups. As of Round 4, several orgs have 'comparator' arms appended to the pitcher list — they show up in the per-org table but the original narrative writeups describe the originally-analyzed arms; see the table for the full sample.

MIL.rhythm: Standard 6-day rotation rhythm — strictly adhered to across the originally-analyzed arms (Birchard, Meccage, Knoth).
MIL.cap: Pitch counts scale to level and experience: Birchard (High-A, established 22yo) up to 92P max, Knoth (Low-A, prep arm 18yo) 85P max, Meccage (Low-A, injury-disrupted 19yo) 82P max.
MIL.buildup: Fast ramp for new pros (Meccage +3.8, Knoth +2.5), flat for established (Birchard already at 70s+ from Day 1).
MIL.short: No skipped turns even after bad outings. Sends pitchers back on normal 6-day rest regardless of previous outcome. Bounce-backs at 85-115% of pre-short pitch count.
MIL.injury: Meccage's 9/3 return was 14P / 1 IP after a 33-day absence — consistent with a limited first-outing-back pattern, though the CSV alone can't confirm this was a formal org protocol vs. an ad-hoc call.
MIL.risk: Knoth's TJ (January 2025) had NO pitch count warning signs visible in the 2024 data. His ACWR never spiked above 1.25, pitch count range was 53-85P all year, and his final 5 starts showed no decline. This is a critical caveat for workload-based injury models — mechanical/tissue-level issues can be invisible in counting stats.
MIL.strengths: Disciplined rest cycles, caps scaled to individual player profile, measured return-to-play pattern observed (Meccage 9/3 return). Round-4 expansion (Corniel, M Rodriguez, Torres) gives MIL the deepest sample in the dataset (n=6) — see the per-pitcher table for individual profiles.
MIL.concerns: No pre-injury detection mechanism visible in volume data. Birchard's chronic workload shows signs of running hot in the middle of the year.

SEA.rhythm: 7-day rotation rhythm, religiously enforced. Sloan had 19 of 20 rest gaps at exactly 7 days. Ford had 20 of 22 at exactly 7.
SEA.cap: Hard caps scale with age/experience: Sloan (19yo, prep arm) capped at 72P; Ford (20yo, 2-year pro) at 89P. The gap reflects year of experience, not org inconsistency — both at Low-A Modesto, same protocol discipline.
SEA.buildup: Sloan = strict fast ramp (+5.9P/start, peaked at start #8). Ford = flat buildup (+1.2P/start), already at 75P on Day 1 from 2024 carryover.
SEA.short: No reported skipped turns. Post-short bounce-backs on normal 7-day rest. Sloan's worst short starts came AFTER the All-Star break extra-rest mini-break and a season-ending IL stint — pattern suggests rust from inactivity, not overuse.
SEA.injury: Sloan ended 2025 on 7-day IL 9/8 (eye procedure later). Ford had no reported injuries. Cijntje's unusual workload is a role issue, not an injury.
SEA.cijntje_special: Jurrangelo Cijntje occupied a piggyback/tandem deployment through April and early May (switch-pitcher novelty). Starts came every 3-4 days at highly variable pitch counts (15P to 87P). Piggyback is still a starter role — normal rest, similar prep, just slotted behind an opener — so the phase itself isn't a 'role change' in any workload-relevant sense. What it DOES break is ACWR interpretation: his 3-start chronic baseline sat in the 30-50P range during this phase, and ACWR ratios become mathematically volatile when the denominator is low (small absolute pitch-count increases register as large ratios). His three ACWR spikes above 1.5 all landed here, and all read as metric artifacts rather than workload overload signals. From 5/18/25 forward he moved into normal starter rotation cadence and the ACWR smooths out. Promoted to AA Arkansas on 8/9/25 and hit season-high 99P on 9/3 — caps expanded at the higher level.
SEA.strengths: Rest discipline is the tightest in the orgs sampled here (n=3). Ford's 100% in-band ACWR is the highest in the dataset. Caps climb across the three sampled pitchers — though with age and level climbing together across Sloan (19, Low-A) → Ford (20, Low-A) → Cijntje (21, AA), we can't cleanly separate 'scales with age' from 'scales with level' from this sample.
SEA.concerns: Sloan's end-of-season IL suggests accumulated wear — worth monitoring how much of the strict 7-day rhythm left him unable to absorb a deload.
SEA.cross_finding: 30-org population: SEA ranks 1st of 30 orgs in pitches-per-inning efficiency (15.82 P/IP) — consistent with our per-pitcher read of strict pitch-count caps and high inning volume. 33% same-org return rate (2/6) with a small cohort; the two returners averaged +46.5 IP gain — when SEA arms do come back, they carry significantly more volume.

NYM.rhythm: 6-day primary rhythm, occasional 5-day compression. Some 7-day extended rest around promotions.
NYM.cap: Low-A/High-A caps around 80-85P, bumped to 90+ at Double-A for high-end prospects. McLean hit 92P at Binghamton late in 2024 as his MLB debut approached.
NYM.buildup: Steady climbers (+2.5-2.7 P/start). Both Santucci and McLean show clean incremental progressions. No dramatic early-season jumps.
NYM.short: Bounce-back immediate in pitch-count terms. Santucci's 7/19 short outing (1.2 IP / 46P) was followed a week later by 5.2 IP / 78P — 111% of the pre-short pitch count. No tempering on re-entry.
NYM.injury: Neither reported significant injury. McLean made his MLB debut in 2025 — clean health history through this window.
NYM.promo: Clear pitch count increase after AA promotion. Santucci's average P went from ~73 at Brooklyn to ~80 at Binghamton.
NYM.strengths: Clean ACWR profile for both (91-96% sweet spot). Measured progression that still results in MLB-ready arms by age 22-23.
NYM.concerns: Santucci's 7/19 short outing came after the shortest All-Star break (+2d) in the dataset. The CSV alone can't tell us whether the light break contributed, whether it was opponent/matchup-driven, or whether it was unrelated — one event isn't enough to attribute causation.
NYM.cross_finding: 30-org population: NYM is the most conservative same-org year-over-year builder in the dataset (-3.9 IP avg for same-org repeat pairs, 5 pairs) — aligning with the measured, unhurried per-start progression we see in Santucci and McLean. Strict return rate is 18% (2/11), which looks low, but both our featured pitchers reached MLB within a year of their sampled seasons — exits from the minors aren't failures of the org's process here.

ATL.rhythm: 7-day rotation base, with occasional compression to 3-5 days (Baumann had a 3-day rest compressed outing in early July 2024). Promotions handled with 8-11 day transition gaps.
ATL.cap: Highest ceiling in the 18-22yo range among orgs sampled. Caminiti pushed to 93P by 18-19yo. Hackenberg regularly hit 90-97P across all three levels. Baumann capped around 85P in Low-A.
ATL.buildup: Caminiti = fast ramp (+5.3P/start) from 41P to 73P in 7 starts. Hackenberg opened at 70P already built (college arm). Baumann +2.1P/start steady.
ATL.short: Caminiti had no qualifying short starts across 17 outings — all comebacks on normal rest. Baumann's 5/25 short start (1 IP/32P) was followed by normal 7-day rest and a 65P outing.
ATL.injury: None of the three showed significant injury patterns. Caminiti had pre-season forearm tendinitis but managed through with FCL tune-ups.
ATL.multi_level: Hackenberg's 2024 is the MULTI-LEVEL exemplar of this study: Rome (High-A, 12 GS) → Mississippi (AA, 9 GS) → Gwinnett (AAA, 4 GS). 25 GS, 129 IP, max 97P, 91% ACWR sweet spot. Transitions handled with 8-11 day gaps between levels. This is how you get a college arm from Low-A to AAA in 10 months without breaking him.
ATL.strengths: Progressive buildup discipline across the 3 sampled pitchers. Hackenberg was promoted through three levels in one season and maintained a clean ACWR profile throughout. Caminiti's 100% ACWR sweet spot shows it applies to prep arms in this sample too.
ATL.concerns: End-of-season 92-93P in 18-19yo Caminiti is aggressive compared to the 18-19 group average (82P) — but it's a single pitcher at that age in ATL, so read it as 'this one arm was pushed hard in his first year,' not 'ATL systematically pushes young arms.' Three pitchers isn't a complete picture.
ATL.cross_finding: 30-org population: ATL's strict same-org return rate is 27% (3/11) — middle of the pack among orgs in this analysis. Year-over-year IP trend is +5.5 IP avg (5 pairs), modest but positive. P/IP efficiency ranks 12th of 30 (16.39). The population read is consistent with the per-pitcher picture: ATL is measured, not aggressive, and not losing arms at an unusual rate.

TB.rhythm: 7-day rhythm with flexibility — multiple 6-day compressed outings, several 8-day extensions. Less rigid than SEA but still disciplined. Willing to skip a turn (Harrison is the dataset's only confirmed skip).
TB.cap: Aggressive caps — Harrison (20yo) hit 92P five times, Gill Hill (21yo) hit 92P, Nichols (21yo) hit 87P before his injury stints. 85-92P range is the TB working ceiling for the originally-analyzed group.
TB.buildup: Fast ramp (Harrison +4.5P/start). Gill Hill smoother steady (+2.8). Nichols interrupted build by injuries.
TB.short: Harrison is the ONLY pitcher in the original 24-pitcher dataset with a confirmed skipped turn after a short start (7/11/25 → 7/23/25). Nichols had multiple short starts driven by injury stints.
TB.injury: Nichols had THREE IL stints in 2024 with a 'nagging injury' never publicly disclosed — same pattern as Meyer (MIA 2024). He bounced back in 2025 (14-3, promoted to AA), but the 2024 protocol of repeated short IL visits without resolving the underlying issue is a cautionary tale. Harrison's 12-day gap around the All-Star Break window was the longest ASB-adjacent rest in the dataset (+5d vs the league norm).
TB.strengths: Workhorse deployment for healthy arms (Harrison, Gill Hill both around 110 IP). Harrison's skipped turn after a short start is the only confirmed one in the original 24-pitcher sample. Round-4 added Urbina (2025 Low-A→High-A) and S Suarez (2024 Low-A) as comparators — see table for full TB sample.
TB.concerns: Nichols 2024 is the case study. Three IL stints without clear resolution, continuing to run him out at 45-79P in between. Eventually his body got right in 2025, but this is the 'push through it' playbook that carries long-term risk. Same signature as Meyer's 2024 MIA season.
TB.cross_finding: 30-org population: TB is the most aggressive same-org year-over-year builder in the dataset (+18.5 IP avg, orgs with 5+ repeat pairs) — independently confirming what the per-pitcher ACWR data shows. TB also ranks 3rd of 30 in pitches-per-inning efficiency (15.88 P/IP), suggesting that when their pitchers work, they work efficiently; the aggression shows up in volume escalation across seasons, not in batters-faced-per-pitch within them.

CLE.rhythm: Primarily 6-day rotation, very consistent. Occasional 5-6 day compressions. Messick had some 5-day short rest at AA, handled fine.
CLE.cap: Dramatic intra-org range in the originally-analyzed arms: Doughty (19yo, Low-A) capped at 80P all year; Messick (23yo, High-A→AA) pushed to 97P regularly. 17P difference between a prep arm and a college arm 4 years further developed.
CLE.buildup: Doughty = flattest build in dataset (+1.2P/start). Messick = steady workhorse (+2.3P/start), opened at 76P.
CLE.short: Doughty's short-start bounce-backs on normal rest. Messick's 8/1/24 outing (1 IP/23P) was followed by normal 5-day rest and an 80P outing (102% of pre-short).
CLE.injury: Neither pitcher had reported injury disruptions in these seasons.
CLE.exemplar: Parker Messick's 2024 is the 'covers innings cleanly' exemplar. 28 GS, 138.3 IP, max 97P, 80% ACWR sweet spot (slightly dragged down by his two short outings in late July/early August but otherwise textbook). Promoted Lake County → Akron on 6/22/24. Eastern League All-Star. 2025 at AAA. MLB debut April 2026 with a near-no-hitter in his 11th start. Path: 2022 draft → 2023 Low-A → 2024 full HA/AA → 2025 AAA → 2026 MLB. Three full 121+ IP seasons before debut.
CLE.strengths: Two pitchers at opposite ends of the developmental spectrum, both well-managed. Messick is the workhorse model; Doughty is the tight-leash prep model. Round-4 added Humphries (2024 High-A) and Hernandez (2025 High-A) as comparators.
CLE.concerns: Doughty only cleared 70P in 5 of 22 outings, didn't break 72P until start #10. The 'tight leash' protects short-term but potentially under-builds volume capacity.

CLE/WAS.rhythm: 6-day rhythm in both systems. Smooth transition post-trade — no disrupted rest cycle.
CLE/WAS.cap: VERY tight 80P cap all year. Never exceeded 80P across 27 starts spanning two organizations. Shared protocol or coincidence? Both CLE (pre-7/29) and WAS (post-7/29) capped him identically.
CLE/WAS.buildup: Flat from Day 1 (61P opener, peaked at 80P). 19yo LHP prep arm — very conservative in the volume department.
CLE/WAS.short: One short start (8/3 at 0.2 IP/33P) right after the trade — followed by normal rest and 70P outing 7 days later.
CLE/WAS.injury: No reported injury in 2024. Won Carolina League Championship with Fredericksburg.
CLE/WAS.strengths: Remarkable consistency across two organizations. 27 GS, 100 IP, 80P max, 88% ACWR sweet. Protocol-adherent organizations regardless of org logo.
CLE/WAS.concerns: Small sample (one pitcher across two orgs).

NYY/CHW.rhythm: Pre-trade: 7-day rhythm at Tampa (Low-A). Post-trade: 6-day rhythm at Winston-Salem (High-A) — tighter compression.
NYY/CHW.cap: Pre-trade ceiling around 87-93P building through June (93P on 6/25). Post-trade fell to 60-85P range with one 44P planned outing in first game back.
NYY/CHW.buildup: Pre-trade: smooth fast ramp (+4.6P/start), 46 → 87 across first 10 starts. Post-trade buildup disrupted by the 8/9 first-day-in-new-org 1 IP / 36P outing.
NYY/CHW.short: Post-trade 8/9 start was 1 IP / 36P — a short first-outing-in-a-new-org. The CSV alone can't tell us if this was a planned low-volume reintroduction or a chased start. Followed by normal 6-day rest and 79P outing next time out (ACWR spike to 1.63).
NYY/CHW.injury: Trade-related reset, not injury. Ziehl's three ACWR spikes above 1.5 (8/15, 8/21, 8/27) all came in the rebuild phase following the 8/9 short-outing debut in the new org.
NYY/CHW.strengths: Pre-trade development at Tampa was clean — NYY had him on a proper ramp.
NYY/CHW.concerns: Trade during the season creates workload discontinuity that's unavoidable but worth flagging. CHW's first move was to reset him with a low-volume outing, which is responsible, but the rapid ramp back up in August pushed his ACWR into the danger zone three times.

NYY.rhythm: 7-day rhythm at High-A Hudson Valley for both. Hess moved to 5-6 day tighter rotation at AA Somerset after 8/5 promotion.
NYY.cap: Both of NYY's 2024 college draftees hit 94-99P in their 2025 first pro season (n=2, directional). Cunningham pushed to 92P+ in 5 of 7 pre-injury starts. Hess peaked at 99P at High-A, 95P at AA.
NYY.buildup: Cunningham opened at 76P already built (college arm) and climbed fast to 92-94P by start #3. Hess similar aggressive build — hit 97-99P by starts 6-8.
NYY.short: Cunningham's post-injury ramp-up (30-49P range Aug-Sept) is rehab-mode, not true short-start protocol. Hess had one short start (5/29 at 4.1 IP/99P was pushed deep, then 27-day gap, return at 1.2 IP/42P).
NYY.injury: Both had injury-related disruptions. Cunningham: 65+ day shoulder IL (6/9 → 8/13), plus an earlier April IL stint, plus a 2026 Opening Day IL. Worst injury story in the 2025 cohort. Hess: 27-day gap in June without formal IL placement, injury-prone history (flexor strain 2023, HS stress fracture).
NYY.pattern: Two NYY 2024 draftees, both college, both pushed to 94-99P in their first pro year, both with mid-season injury disruptions. With n=2 the picture is small-sample noisy in both directions: the in-band ACWR rate for the two healthy windows reads tight, but both arms also lost meaningful chunks of season to injury. The full 2025 60+ IP MiLB population has 9 NYY arms in it — see the league-position chips at the top of this page for where NYY actually sits across that wider sample (not where the 2-pitcher in-band rate alone would put it).
NYY.cross_finding: 30-org population: NYY has the lowest strict same-org return rate of any org with a meaningful cohort — 0 of 9 qualifying pitchers returned to the same org the following year with more IP. Trades, MLB promotions, and injuries all collapse into that 0/9 figure, so we can't separate causal strands from this data alone. But paired with the visible workload signals in our 2-pitcher sample (94-99P first pro years, two mid-season disruptions), the combination warrants forward monitoring rather than dismissal.
NYY.strengths: When healthy and short-window, both showed quick development curves and ability to work deep into games early. Hess's AA finish (2.70 ERA over 7 starts) shows the plan can work when health holds.
NYY.concerns: 94-99P ceiling in a first pro season is aggressive, and both sampled pitchers had mid-season disruptions. With n=2 plus injury history we can't cleanly attribute the shoulder IL or the 27-day gap to pitch-count aggression specifically — Hess carried injury history from before the draft (flexor strain 2023, HS stress fracture). Read the small-sample in-band rate alongside the league-position chips above; the 2-pitcher healthy-window numbers can read tighter than the org's broader pattern.

LAD.rhythm: 6-day rhythm most consistent. Both originally-analyzed pitchers show occasional 5-day compressions and 7-8 day extensions.
LAD.cap: Tightest caps in the originally-analyzed sample (n=2, directional). Patick peaked at 75P across 25 starts; Zazueta peaked at 76P across 19 starts. Both pitchers are 19-20yo in Low-A or Rookie/Low-A, so the 75-80P ceiling could be age-and-level appropriate rather than an org-wide choice — we can't separate those with two pitchers both in the young/low-level band. Ferris (Round-4 comparator) reaches further up the chain (High-A → AA) and his table row gives a counterpoint.
LAD.buildup: Both steady builds (+2.0-2.3 P/start). Patick's early-season pitch count was chaotic (51→26 across 4 starts), then stabilized. Zazueta smooth ramp from complex league into Rancho.
LAD.short: Patick is the outlier example — his 4/26 outing of 0.1 IP / 26P was followed by a normal 51P next time (pre-short pattern). LAD does not appear to temper pitch counts after a short outing in this 2-pitcher sample.
LAD.injury: Zazueta was named 2025 Dodgers MiLPOY after a Rancho season capped at ~70 IP total — his workload was held to a tight cap throughout. A 14-day gap in 2024 mid-season is consistent with planned tempering, though the CSV alone can't confirm org intent. Patick: no reported injury but late-season average P declined, consistent with a planned wind-down.
LAD.strengths: Workload discipline is strict within the originally-analyzed sample. Zazueta was named 2025 Dodgers MiLPOY after Rancho season with ~70 IP total — they're willing to hold back even productive arms. Clean ACWR profiles for both (81-86% sweet).
LAD.concerns: The 75P ceiling could be under-building volume capacity if LAD holds this line at High-A and above. Ferris was added as a Round-4 comparator at High-A → AA — see his row for a higher-level data point.

MIA.rhythm: 7-day rotation, disciplined. Both originally-analyzed pitchers averaged ~7 day rest across the season.
MIA.cap: High-ceiling caps for young arms — White hit 86P, Meyer 85P in the same org during 2024. Both at Jupiter Low-A, then Beloit High-A.
MIA.buildup: White was a steady climber (+2.1P/start) who opened at 71P and never dropped below 68P all year. Meyer was flat (+1.2P/start) but erratic due to injury.
MIA.short: White had ZERO qualifying short starts across 21 outings — the most consistent pitcher in the dataset. Meyer had MULTIPLE short starts driven by injury.
MIA.injury: MIA had the most dramatic intra-org split. White: zero injuries, zero short starts, 100% ACWR sweet spot — textbook clean season. Meyer: nagging undisclosed injury through most of High-A tenure, two 18-21 day gaps without formal IL placement.
MIA.strengths: When things go well, Miami's plan is the cleanest in the originally-analyzed dataset — White's 2024 is a gold standard. Consistent rest, scaled caps, smooth progression. L Martinez (Round-4 comparator, 2025 High-A international arm) adds a third data point — see table.
MIA.concerns: Meyer case is a warning. His 2024 shows a pattern of trying to rest through an injury without formal IL placement — two long gaps but no transaction entry. The org kept running him out. Same signature as Nichols's 2024 TB season.
MIA.cross_finding: 30-org population: MIA leads all 30 orgs in strict same-org return rate — 58% (7 of 12 qualifying pitcher-seasons came back to the same org with more IP the following year). That's the strongest population-level validation of the sustainable-management read: the arms MIA builds up per-start appear to actually hold up and carry more work year over year. One tension: MIA ranks 25th of 30 in pitches-per-inning (16.81 P/IP) — their pitchers throw more pitches per inning than most orgs, even while the per-start rhythm looks disciplined.

DET.rhythm: Mixed across the four-arm sample (2022, 2024×2, 2025). Lakeland and West Michigan rotations show 5-7 day rest with intermittent compressions; no single rigid pattern across the four pitchers.
DET.cap: Variable. Jobe's 2022 (top draft prospect, 19yo, High-A→Low-A reassignment) peaked in the mid-80s. Sears 2024 (High-A) and Hamm 2024 (Low-A) sit in the 80-90P working range. Elissalt 2025 (High-A→Low-A) reads tighter — see per-pitcher detail.
DET.buildup: Per-arm — see individual pitcher pages. The four-arm sample spans three years and three levels, which limits within-org pattern claims; the league-position chips above contextualize where DET's 2025 60+ IP MiLB cohort sits among the 30 orgs.
DET.short: Per-arm — see individual pitcher pages. No standout short-start pattern visible across the four arms.
DET.injury: Jobe's 2022 was his first full pro season; subsequent injury history (multiple IL stints in 2023-2024) is documented elsewhere but the 2022 CSV reads as a clean season at the time.
DET.pattern: DET is the org-of-interest for this project (the project author works for the Detroit Tigers, included transparently as a comparator). The four-arm sample crosses 2022-2025 and is intentionally mixed-year to give time-series depth rather than a single-season org snapshot. Read the per-pitcher pages alongside the league-position chips at the top of this section.
DET.strengths: Sample now spans both ends of the prospect spectrum (Jobe was a #3 overall draft pick; Sears/Hamm/Elissalt are mid-round and international comparators). Multi-year coverage allows for org-process-evolution observation.
DET.concerns: Mixed-year sample (2022-2025) limits clean year-over-year pattern attribution. Background distribution is also mixed (1 prep, 1 college, 1 international, 1 unknown). Read per-arm; treat the org-level rollup with the same small-sample skepticism applied elsewhere.
DET.cross_finding: 30-org population: DET has the 2nd-best strict same-org return rate in the dataset (56%, 5 of 9) and ranks 4th of 30 in pitches-per-inning efficiency (15.91 P/IP). This sits in tension with the per-pitcher ACWR tier placement (most aggressive sampled, 63.8% sweet) — but isn't contradictory. The ACWR metric captures within-season rhythmic inconsistency; the return rate captures multi-season durability outcomes. DET's below-average per-start pitch counts (24/30) may allow arms to accumulate volume sustainably across seasons even when individual-start ACWR runs noisier. Also of note: the 30-org study flagged DET as having the best weighted xPitchRV/P among orgs whose pitchers were younger than their level context — young-for-level and still effective.

BOS.cap: Per-arm. Paez 2023 at Salem (Low-A), full season at one level.
BOS.injury: No major injury disruption in Paez's 2023 record.
BOS.pattern: First BOS entry in the dataset (Round-4 comparator). Single-pitcher sample — directional only. League-position chips above contextualize where BOS sits across the 2025 60+ IP MiLB cohort regardless of how thin our own sample is.
BOS.strengths: Adds a previously-unrepresented org to the sample.
BOS.concerns: Single pitcher; no within-org pattern can be drawn from n=1.

STL.rhythm: 6-7 day rotation rhythm across the four arms. No compressed back-to-back patterns in the data.
STL.cap: Progressive caps by age and level: Rincon (Low-A debut) built up to ~95P by end of 2023 season; Hansen operates in the 80-90P range at AA; McGreevy and Graceffo reached 95-100P+ at AAA. STL is not a conservative-cap org — it trusts its development track.
STL.buildup: Patient multi-year ramps are the signature. Rincon 49 IP (ROK) → 53 IP → 123 IP → 130 IP → 87 IP — a 3-year build before the first 100+ IP season. McGreevy 7 IP (draft year) → 141 IP → 150 IP → 146 IP. The org absorbs partial-year entries and doesn't rush year-one totals.
STL.injury: Graceffo's 2023 was notably shortened (78 IP at AAA) after a full 136 IP 2022 breakout — likely injury-related, though STL never confirmed publicly. His 2024 comeback (124 IP) shows recovery management. Otherwise the four arms are notable for their multi-year consecutive season presence — a strong health signal.
STL.graduation: McGreevy reached MLB in 2025 (171 IP across majors/minors). He and Graceffo are among the most-IP arms in this dataset's MLBers list. Hansen and Rincon are on-deck at AA/AAA as of 2025-2026.
STL.arc: STL's most distinctive feature is the velocity arc: Rincon gained +7.1 mph from his DSL debut (87.6 mph, 2021) to his 2025 AAA-level starts (94.7 mph). McGreevy's velo remained steady (91.3 → 93.3 mph) across five seasons — the consistency mark. Graceffo's velo dipped from 94.8 in his 2022 breakout to 92.5 in his 2024 comeback — a yellow flag worth monitoring. Hansen shows slow but steady velo gain (90.1 → 90.9 mph) across three seasons, consistent with a groundball-profile innings-eater.
STL.strengths: The strongest multi-season development dataset in this study. Four arms with 3-6 years of game logs, multiple of whom appear on the MLBers 200+IP list. STL's homegrown track record — not just acquiring arms, but building them from Low-A through MLB — is the clearest case-study answer to the STL exploitation hypothesis: these arms show consecutive-season presence, progressive IP, and no dramatic workload spikes in the data.
STL.concerns: Hence's 2025-2026 walk spike (BB% up from 8-9% in 2023-2024 to 11.5%+ in 2025) is the one active concern in the STL orbit. His 2026 partial season is too small to draw conclusions but worth tracking. Graceffo's velo dip post-injury also warrants monitoring as he enters his late-development window.
STL.cross_finding: MLBers 200+IP list: STL has 5 arms on the cumulative 200+IP list (McGreevy, Graceffo, and others acquired). The homegrown arms (McGreevy, Graceffo, Rincon) account for 3 of those — the development system is producing durable starters, not just acquiring them.
